summary: Jeff Atwood dissects the gaming of Time Magazine's 2009 World's Most Influential People poll, revealing a cascade of basic security failures. The poll launched with no authentication, no input validation, and easily bypassed rate limiting. Time's attempts to fix the vulnerabilities were equally amateur, hiding a 'secret' salt in client-side Flash code. The result was hackers not only putting 4chan founder moot at the top but spelling out a message with the first letters of the top 21 names. Atwood argues this wasn't impressive hacking—it was shooting fish in a barrel against incompetent developers.
key_points: Time's poll launched without authentication, validation, or even range checking on vote values—basic web security 101 failures
Their attempted fix hid the authentication salt in client-side Flash, which hackers quickly extracted
The 13-second IP throttling could be bypassed by simply cycling through different candidates
The 'hackers' spelled out 'marblecake also the game' using the first letters of the top 21 names
Atwood dismisses this as not a real hack, contrasting it with genuinely impressive exploits like the Black Sunday hack
money_quotes: This isn't high security; this is web 101.
Any programmer with even a rudimentary understanding of how the web works would have thought of these exploits immediately.
When good is dumb, evil will always triumph.
Is it really a precision hack when your adversaries are incompetent?
Time eventually responded – with all the skill and expertise of... a team who put together the world's most insecure online poll.
So-called secret poorly hidden on the client: check!
I'm not sure they come any dumber than these clowns.
key_insight: Exploiting a system with no security isn't hacking—it's walking through an open door, and the real failure belongs to developers who ignore basic web security principles.
